metadata.name: 3.A.1 Number of physicians per 10 thous. population (employed persons in the main place of work)
metadata.priority: Increasing access to public health services
metadata.definition: Number of physicians (working directly with a patient) per 10 thous. population.
metadata.methodology: Pursuant to the Act of 5 December 1996 on the Profession of Doctor and Dentist (unified text Journal of Laws 2005 No 226, item 1943), a doctor is a person having required qualifications, confirmed with appropriate documents to perform the profession of a doctor, i.e. who provides health services, and in particular examines health state, diagnoses diseases and prevents them, leads treatment and rehabilitation of patients, provides consultations, as well as issues medical statements and opinions. Pursuant to Act of 5 December 1996 on the Profession of Doctor and Dentist, the profession of doctor can be practised according to various legal forms. Dentists are not included as doctors. Persons working directly with a patient - persons indicated as working in at least one of the sources: Register of Entities Performing Medical Activity, list of entities having a contract with National Health Fund, entities from the Statistical Units Base, operating according to the Polish Classification of Activity in Section Q Health and social work or active practice (individual or group). The following criteria are used in determining the main place of work for people working with a patient: • if a person works in one place, this is their main place of work; • if a person works in more than one workplace, the main workplace is considered to be the one where the working time is the greatest; • another criterion for choosing the main place of work was: the basis for assessing the pension insurance contribution, and if it is impossible to use it - the basis for assessing the health insurance contribution; • if the above, successively applied criteria do not allow for an unambiguous choice, the main place of work is considered to be the one where the person working in the profession of a doctor or dentist or nurse or midwife is employed under an employment contract (i.e. where the first four the digits of the insurance title code are 01 10); • in other cases, the main place of work is determined randomly.

metadata.name: 3.A.2 Number of dentists per 10 thous. population (employed persons in the main place of work)
metadata.priority: Increasing access to public health services
metadata.definition: Number of dentists (working according to their primary place of work) per 10 thousand people.
metadata.methodology: A dentist is a medical profession that involves the provision of health services in the field of diseases of the teeth, oral cavity, facial part of the skull and adjacent areas by a person with the required qualifications, confirmed by appropriate documents. The regional medical council grants the right to practice medicine to a person who has obtained the required qualifications, completed a postgraduate internship and passed the Medical-Dental State Examination.Employed according to the primary place of work are people working full-time, in hours exceeding full-time and part-time in relation to the working time specified in the applicable regulations for a given type of work. These people, regardless of the working time, are counted once, according to the primary place of employment.

metadata.name: 3.A.3 Number of nurses and midwives per 10 thous. population (employed persons in the main place of work)
metadata.priority: Increasing access to public health services
metadata.definition: Number of nurses and midwives (working directly with a patient) per 10 thous. population.
metadata.methodology: A nurse - a person who has required qualifications, confirmed by appropriate documents, to perform the profession of a nurse, which consists of providing health services (in particular as regards nursing, preventive, diagnostic, medical, rehabilitative and health promotion services. (Act of 5 July 1996 on the Profession of Nurses and Midwives - consolidated text: Journal of Laws of 2009, No. 151, item 1217, with subsequent amendments). A midwife - a person who has required qualifications, confirmed by appropriate documents, to perform the profession of a midwife, which consists of providing health services (in particular as regards nursing, preventive, diagnostic, medical, rehabilitative a and health promotion services, connected with care over a woman, a pregnant woman, a childbearing woman, a woman lying-in as well as over an infant). (Act of 5 July 1996 on the Profession of Nurses and Midwives. Original text : Journal of Laws 1996 No 91, item 410 consolidated text: Journal of Laws of 2001, No. 57, item 602, with later amendments). Persons working directly with a patient - persons indicated as working in at least one of the sources: Register of Entities Performing Medical Activity, list of entities having a contract with National Health Fund, entities from the Statistical Units Base, operating according to the Polish Classification of Activity in Section Q Health and social work or active practice (individual or group). The following criteria are used in determining the main place of work for people working with a patient: • if a person works in one place, this is their main place of work; • if a person works in more than one workplace, the main workplace is considered to be the one where the working time is the greatest; • another criterion for choosing the main place of work was: the basis for assessing the pension insurance contribution, and if it is impossible to use it - the basis for assessing the health insurance contribution; • if the above, successively applied criteria do not allow for an unambiguous choice, the main place of work is considered to be the one where the person working in the profession of a doctor or dentist or nurse or midwife is employed under an employment contract (i.e. where the first four the digits of the insurance title code are 01 10); • in other cases, the main place of work is determined randomly.

metadata.name: 3.A.4 Emergency rescue teams per 100 thous. population
metadata.priority: Increasing access to public health services
metadata.definition: Emergency medical services units per 100 thous. population.
metadata.methodology: The emergency medical system unit is tasked with undertaking medical rescue operations for any person in a state of sudden threat to life or health. The system units are hospital emergency departments and emergency medical teams, including air medical rescue teams.

metadata.name: 3.A.5 Number of patients in nursing homes per 10 thous. population
metadata.priority: Increasing access to public health services
metadata.definition: Number of patients in nursing homes per 10 thous. population.
metadata.methodology: Nursing homesHealth care unit or organizational unit of the health care unit, in which the therapeutic entity conducts in-patient and 24-hour medical services in kind of other than hospital services. These services may rely in particular on providing 24-hour health care services which cover nursing and rehabilitation of patients who do not require hospitalization and providing them with pharmaceutical agents needed for continuation of a treatment, accommodation and food appropriate to their health condition and also conducting health education for patients and members of their families and preparation these patients for self-care.

metadata.name: 3.A.6 Number of patients in chronic medical care homes per 10 thous. Population
metadata.priority: Increasing access to public health services
metadata.definition: Number of patients in care and treatment facilities per 10 thousand people.
metadata.methodology: A care and treatment facility is a medical facility of a medical entity or an organizational unit of a medical facility in which the medical entity performs medical activities such as stationary and 24-hour health services other than hospital services. The services may consist in particular in providing 24-hour health services, which include care and rehabilitation of patients who do not require hospitalization, and providing them with medicinal products and medical devices, premises and food appropriate to their health condition, as well as conducting health education for patients and members of their families and preparing these people for self-care and self-care at home.

metadata.name: 3.A.7 Number of patients in hospices per 10 thous. Population
metadata.priority: Increasing access to public health services
metadata.definition: Number of patients in hospices per 10 thousand population.
metadata.methodology: A hospice is a medical facility of a medical entity or an organizational unit of a medical facility in which the medical entity performs medical activities such as stationary and 24-hour health services other than hospital services. The services may consist in particular in providing comprehensive health, psychological and social care for patients in a terminal condition and care for the families of such patients.

metadata.name: 3.B.1 Number of beds in hospices, chronic medical care homes and nursing homes per 100 thous. population
metadata.priority: Improving the quality and availability of healthcare infrastructure
metadata.definition: Number of beds in hospices, chronic medical care homes and nursing homes per 100 thous. population.
metadata.methodology: Bed – bed placed in the sick room which is regularly maintained and staffed and immediately available for the care of admitted patients. Beds in general hospitals do not include day-care beds, the beds (couches) included in the diagnostic, delivery, dialysis sites, etc. as well as auxiliary beds on which a patient is temporarily for instance post-procedure recovery beds.A hospice, a chronic medical care home and a nursing home are health care unit or organizational unit of the health care unit, in which the therapeutic entity conducts inpatient medical activity and 24-hour medical services other than hospital services.The hospice provides services consisting in particular of comprehensive health care, psychological and social care over patients in terminal phase of disease as well as over a families of these patients.Chronic medical care homes provides services consisting in particular in providing 24-hour health care services which cover the scope of nursing and rehabilitation of patients who do not require hospitalization and providing them with pharmaceutical agents and medical materials, accommodation and food appropriate to their health condition and also conducting health education for patients and members of their families and preparation these patients for self-care and personal care at home.Nursing homes provides services consisting in particular on providing 24-hour health care services which cover nursing and rehabilitation of patients who do not require hospitalization and providing them with pharmaceutical agents needed for continuation of a treatment, accommodation and food appropriate to their health condition and also conducting health education for patients and members of their families and preparation these patients for self-care.

metadata.name: 3.B.2 Average number of beds in health resort hospital and health resort sanatoria
metadata.priority: Improving the quality and availability of healthcare infrastructure
metadata.definition: Average number of beds in spa hospitals and spa sanatoriums.
metadata.methodology: The average number of beds is the sum of the number of beds as of the end of each month divided by 12, i.e. the number of months in a year. A spa hospital is a spa treatment facility whose tasks include providing a patient who has been referred for spa treatment or spa rehabilitation with: 1) 24-hour health care services in inpatient conditions; 2) 24-hour medical and nursing care; 3) procedures specified in the treatment program; 4) use of natural medicinal resources and spa treatment facilities; 5) health education. A spa sanatorium is a spa treatment facility. The tasks of a sanatorium include providing a patient who has been referred for spa treatment or spa rehabilitation with: 1) 24-hour health care services in inpatient conditions; 2) medical care and 24-hour nursing care; 3) procedures specified in the treatment program; 4) preventive services; 5) health education.

metadata.name: 3.B.3 Number of out-patients departments per 10 thous. population
metadata.priority: Improving the quality and availability of healthcare infrastructure
metadata.definition: Number of clinics per 10 thousand total population.
metadata.methodology: A clinic is a medical facility of a medical entity that provides outpatient health services, primary or specialist health care, and medical rehabilitation services. The indicator relating to the number and structure of the population from 2010 was recalculated in accordance with the balance sheet prepared based on the results of the 2011 National Census. The indicators relating to the number and structure of the population from 2020 were recalculated in accordance with the balance sheet prepared based on the results of the 2021 National Census.

metadata.name: 3.B.4 Number of beds in hospitals per 10 thous. population
metadata.priority: Improving the quality and availability of healthcare infrastructure
metadata.definition: Number of beds in general hospitals per 10 thousand population.
metadata.methodology: A hospital is a medical facility of a medical entity or a separate organizational unit of a medical facility in which the entity performs medical activities such as stationary and 24-hour hospital health services, excluding medical facilities of entities providing only services with the intention of completing them within a period not exceeding 24 hours (one-day hospitals). The data does not include day hospital activities. Until 2011, the data on general hospitals did not include entities for which the founding body was the Minister of National Defense, the Minister of Justice (prisons) and the Minister of Internal Affairs and Administration. Since 2012, the data on general hospitals include facilities of the Ministry of National Defense and the Ministry of Internal Affairs and Administration, but does not include hospitals in prisons (Ministry of Justice).

metadata.name: 3.C.1 Number of physicians specialists per 10 thous. population
metadata.priority: Increasing access to specialist and therapeutic treatment, with particular emphasis on lifestyle diseases
metadata.definition: Total number of medical specialists per 10 thousand population.
metadata.dimensions: total, anesthesiology and intensive therapy, surgery, respiratory diseases, internal diseases, dermatology and venereology, cardiology, general practitioners, medicine service, neurology, endocrinology, diabetology, ophthalmology, oncology, otolaryngology, paediatrics, obstetrics and gynaecology, psychiatry, radiology and diagnostic imaging
metadata.methodology: A doctor is a medical profession that involves the provision of services by a person with the required qualifications, confirmed by appropriate documents, in particular: health examination, diagnosis of diseases, their prevention, treatment, rehabilitation of patients, providing medical advice, as well as issuing opinions and medical certificates. The performance of the medical profession is also considered to be the performance of research work by a doctor in the field of medical sciences or health promotion and teaching the medical profession. The regional medical council grants the right to practice the medical profession to a person who has obtained the required qualifications, completed a postgraduate internship and passed the Medical State Examination with a positive result. Data comes from the Ministry of Health and the Ministry of Internal Affairs and Administration. Status on December 31

metadata.name: 3.C.2 Number of deaths due to diseases of the circulatory system per 100 thous. population
metadata.priority: Increasing access to specialist and therapeutic treatment, with particular emphasis on lifestyle diseases
metadata.definition: Number of deaths due to circulatory system diseases per 100 thousand population.
metadata.methodology: Death is a permanent, i.e. irreversible cessation of the functions of organs necessary for life (regardless of the period after live birth), as a result of which the vital functions of the entire organism cease. Diseases of the circulatory system - according to the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Health Problems ICD-10 - a disease entity with the symbol I00-I99 - diseases affecting organs and tissues that are part of the circulatory system, in particular the heart, arteries and veins. When compiling statistical data on deaths by cause, the initial cause of death is assumed. The initial cause is considered to be the disease that was the beginning of the disease process that led to death or the injury or poisoning that resulted in death. In medical practice, the cause of death is additionally distinguished: secondary (which is considered to be a disease or injury or the circumstances of an accident - resulting from the initial cause of death) and direct (which is a disease that becomes the final cause of death as a result of a disease, injury or poisoning, or the circumstances of an accident that are the initial and secondary cause of death). In terms of adjudication on the causes of death, since 1997, the principles of the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Health Problems - 10th Revision (ICD-10) have been in force in Poland.

metadata.name: 3.C.3 Number of deaths due to malignant neoplasms per 100 thous. population
metadata.priority: Increasing access to specialist and therapeutic treatment, with particular emphasis on lifestyle diseases
metadata.definition: Total number of cancer deaths per 100 thousand population.
metadata.methodology: Death is a permanent, i.e. irreversible cessation of the functions of organs necessary for life (regardless of the period after live birth), as a result of which the vital functions of the entire organism cease. Neoplasm - according to the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Health Problems ICD-10: disease entity with the symbol C00-D48 - a group of diseases in which the cells of the body divide in an uncontrolled manner, and newly formed cancer cells do not differentiate into typical tissue cells. Loss of control over division is associated with mutations of genes encoding proteins. These mutations cause the cell to respond not at all or inappropriately to signals from the body. When developing statistical data on deaths by cause, the initial cause of death is assumed. The initial cause is considered to be the disease that is the beginning of the disease process that led to death or the injury or poisoning that resulted in death. In medical practice, the cause of death is additionally distinguished: secondary (which is considered to be a disease or injury or the circumstances of an accident - resulting from the initial cause of death) and direct (which is a disease that becomes the final cause of death as a result of a disease, injury or poisoning, or the circumstances of an accident that are the initial and secondary cause of death). In terms of adjudication on the causes of death, since 1997, the principles of the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Health Problems - 10th Revision (ICD-10) have been in force in Poland.

metadata.name: 3.C.4 Number of deaths due to mental and behavioural disorders per 100 thous. population
metadata.priority: Increasing access to specialist and therapeutic treatment, with particular emphasis on lifestyle diseases
metadata.definition: Number of deaths due to mental and behavioral disorders per 100,000 population.
metadata.methodology: Death is a permanent, i.e. irreversible cessation of the functions of organs necessary for life (regardless of the period after live birth), as a result of which the vital functions of the entire organism cease. Mental disorders - difficulties in the social or mental functioning of an individual. Mental disorders (F01-F89 and F99) can be divided into: organic mental disorders (caused by brain damage); mental and behavioral disorders caused by the use of psychoactive substances (e.g. alcoholism); schizophrenia; affective mood disorders (e.g. depression); neurotic, stress-related and somatic disorders (e.g. phobia); behavioral syndromes associated with physiological disorders and physical factors (e.g. bulimia, night terrors); adult personality and behavioral disorders, mental retardation; mental and psychological development disorders (e.g. childhood autism), mental disorders not otherwise specified (NOS). Behavioral disorders - behavioral and emotional disorders that usually begin in childhood and adolescence (ADHD) according to the ICD-10 classification: F90-F98 - the occurrence of abnormal behaviors that are inappropriate for the child's age and inconsistent with social expectations. When compiling statistical data on deaths by cause, the initial cause of death is assumed. The initial cause is considered to be the disease that is the beginning of the disease process that led to death or the injury or poisoning that resulted in death. In medical practice, the cause of death is additionally distinguished: secondary (which is considered to be the disease or injury or the circumstances of the accident - being a consequence of the initial cause of death) and direct (which is the disease that becomes the final cause of death as a result of the disease, injury or poisoning, or the circumstances of the accident being the initial and secondary cause of death). In the field of adjudication of causes of death, since 1997 the principles of the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Health Problems - 10th Revision (ICD-10) have been in force in Poland.

metadata.name: 3.C.5 Number of female deaths due to cervical cancer per 100 thous. women
metadata.priority: Increasing access to specialist and therapeutic treatment, with particular emphasis on lifestyle diseases
metadata.definition: Number of female deaths due to cervical cancer per 100 thousand women.
metadata.methodology: Death is a permanent, i.e. irreversible cessation of the functions of organs necessary for life (regardless of the period after live birth), as a result of which the vital functions of the entire organism cease. Cervical cancer, also known as cervical cancer, is a malignant tumor that develops in the cervix. In the ICD-10 classification, it is designated with the code C53. When compiling statistical data on deaths by cause, the initial cause of death is considered. The initial cause is considered to be the disease that was the beginning of the disease process that led to death, or the injury or poisoning that resulted in death. In medical practice, the cause of death is additionally distinguished: secondary (which is considered to be a disease or injury or the circumstances of an accident - resulting from the initial cause of death) and direct (which is a disease that becomes the final cause of death as a result of a disease, injury or poisoning, or the circumstances of an accident that are the initial and secondary cause of death). In terms of adjudication on the causes of death, since 1997, the principles of the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Health Problems - 10th Revision (ICD-10) have been in force in Poland.

metadata.name: 3.C.6 Number of deaths due to chronic respiratory disease per 100 thous. population
metadata.priority: Increasing access to specialist and therapeutic treatment, with particular emphasis on lifestyle diseases
metadata.definition: Number of deaths due to chronic respiratory disease per 100 thous. population.
metadata.methodology: Death - permanent, irreversible cessation of functions of the essential for life organs, the consequence of which is the cessation of all functions of the whole organism. Chronic respiratory tract disease - according to the International Classification of Diseases ICD -10: diseases marked with symbols J30-J98 - disorders related to the upper and lower respiratory tract. When compiling the data on deaths by cause the initial cause of death is assumed. The initial cause is the disease, which was at the beginning of the morbid process and which caused the death it may be also the injury or the poisoning, which caused the death. Data relating to the judicature on the causes of death are given in accordance with the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems (Revision X). Data on population were compiled on the basis of:  the balances of the residing population in a gmina based on the results of 2021 Population and Housing Census (for data since 2020) for previous years (2010 - 2019) on the basis of the 2011 Population and Housing Census, the registers of the Ministry of Interior - internal and international migration of population for permanent residence (since 2006 the presented data come from the Common Electronic System of Population Register - PESEL), documentation of Civil Status Offices regarding registered marriages, births and deaths.

metadata.name: 3.C.7 Number of deaths due to diseases of the digestive system per 100 thous. population
metadata.priority: Increasing access to specialist and therapeutic treatment, with particular emphasis on lifestyle diseases
metadata.definition: Number of deaths due to digestive system diseases per 100 thousand population.
metadata.methodology: Death is a permanent, i.e. irreversible cessation of the functions of organs necessary for life (regardless of the period after live birth), as a result of which the vital functions of the entire organism cease. Digestive system  – according to the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems (ICD-10) – disease entities coded K00–K93 – disorders affecting organs of the digestive system, in particular the oral cavity, oesophagus, stomach, intestines, liver, gallbladder, and pancreas. When compiling statistical data on deaths by cause, the initial cause of death is considered. The initial cause is considered to be the disease that was the beginning of the disease process that led to death, or the injury or poisoning that resulted in death. In medical practice, the cause of death is additionally distinguished: secondary (which is considered to be a disease or injury or the circumstances of an accident - resulting from the initial cause of death) and direct (which is a disease that becomes the final cause of death as a result of a disease, injury or poisoning, or the circumstances of an accident that are the initial and secondary cause of death). In terms of adjudication on the causes of death, since 1997, the principles of the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Health Problems - 10th Revision (ICD-10) have been in force in Poland.

metadata.name: 3.D.1 Students of health field of education
metadata.priority: High level of education and professional training of medical staff and their availability to patients
metadata.definition: Number of medical students at public and private universities.
metadata.methodology: A student is a person studying at first or second cycle level or uniform master's level. A field of study is a separate part of one or more areas of education, implemented at a university in a manner specified by the education program. A public university is a university established by the state represented by the appropriate authority or public administration body.

metadata.name: 3.D.2 Graduates of health field of education
metadata.priority: High level of education and professional training of medical staff and their availability to patients
metadata.definition: Number of medical graduates from public and private universities.
metadata.methodology: A graduate is a person who has completed the highest programmatic class in a given type of school and received a certificate of completion of this school - applies to graduates in schools covered by the education system. A graduate of studies receives a diploma of completion of studies in a specific field and profile confirming higher education and a professional title: 1. Bachelor's degree, engineer's degree or equivalent confirming higher education at the same level - in the case of first-cycle studies; 2. Master's degree, master of engineering or equivalent confirming higher education at the same level - in the case of second-cycle studies and uniform master's studies. A field of study is a separate part of one or several areas of education, implemented at the university in a manner specified by the education program. A public university is a university established by the state represented by the appropriate authority or public administration body.

metadata.name: 3.D.3 Students of post-secondary schools, including colleges of social workers - health narrow field of education
metadata.priority: High level of education and professional training of medical staff and their availability to patients
metadata.definition: Number of students of post-secondary schools including colleges of social workers with a medical profile.
metadata.methodology: A post-secondary school is an educational institution organized on the basis of the curriculum of a post-primary school, enabling people with general secondary education to obtain a diploma confirming vocational qualifications or qualifications in a profession after passing a vocational examination. The education lasts no longer than 2.5 years. Graduates, after passing an external examination, receive a diploma that specifies vocational qualifications at the level of a technician or skilled worker. In statistics, post-secondary schools also include three-year colleges for social service workers.

metadata.name: 3.D.4 Participants of postgraduate studies in health fields of education
metadata.priority: High level of education and professional training of medical staff and their availability to patients
metadata.definition: Number of participants in postgraduate studies in medical fields.
metadata.methodology: Postgraduate studies are a form of education for which candidates with at least first-degree qualifications are admitted, conducted at a university, a scientific institute of the Polish Academy of Sciences, a research institute or a Medical Centre for Postgraduate Education, ending with obtaining postgraduate qualifications.

metadata.name: 3.D.5 Participants of doctoral and doctoral studies at universities supervised by the minister responsible for health affairs
metadata.priority: High level of education and professional training of medical staff and their availability to patients
metadata.definition: Participants of doctoral and doctoral studies at universities supervised by the minister responsible for health matters.
metadata.methodology: Doctoral studies are studies preparing for obtaining a doctoral degree, conducted by an authorized organizational unit of a university, a research institute of the Polish Academy of Sciences, a research institute or an international research institute operating in the territory of the Republic of Poland established on the basis of separate regulations, to which candidates with second-cycle qualifications are admitted, ending with obtaining third-cycle qualifications. Participants of doctoral studies, including foreigners. Information presented since 2019 in connection with the introduction of a new typology of institutions of the higher education and science system.

metadata.name: 3.E.1 Life expectancy - males
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Life expectancy expresses the average number of years that in the current mortality conditions a new born person is expected to continue to live.
metadata.methodology: Average (mean) life expectancy  is the indicator calculated on the basis of a unified methodology recommended by the WHO. Life expectancy – a basic element of the life table.The life tables consist of several functions of age, mathematically interrelated (including the number of survivors, number of decedents, and probability of dying, life expectancy), describing theoretical process of population extinction as the population ages. Values of these functions are calculated on the basis of the age and sex specific death and population rates, observed during a given period (usually 1 year).Life tables  are also used to assess population state of health. Thanks to the observation of mortality trends on the basis of life tables, demographic projections are possible.

metadata.name: 3.E.10 Number of medical consultations provided in primary health care per capita
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Medical advice provided to patients in primary health care per capita.
metadata.methodology: Medical advice is a health service provided by a physician, dentist or psychologist. Primary health care is a preventive, diagnostic, therapeutic, rehabilitation and nursing health service in the field of general medicine, family medicine and pediatrics, provided as part of outpatient health care.

metadata.name: 3.E.11 Number of medical consultations provided in out-patient health care per capita
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Medical advice provided to patients in outpatient health care per capita.
metadata.methodology: Medical advice is a health service provided by a physician, dentist or psychologist. Outpatient health services are the provision of primary or specialist health care and services in the field of medical rehabilitation, provided in conditions that do not require their provision in a stationary and 24-hour mode in an appropriately equipped, permanent room. The provision of these services may take place on the premises of the enterprise, including in a vehicle intended for the provision of these services, or at the patient's place of stay.

metadata.name: 3.E.12 Number of medical specialised consultations provided in out-patient health care per capita
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Number of specialist consultations provided as part of outpatient health care (in thousands).
metadata.methodology: Advice provided – a service provided in an outpatient setting, including at home by a doctor, dentist or psychologist. Outpatient health care involves healthcare services provided by service providers to people who do not require treatment in 24-hour or all-day conditions. Guaranteed services in the scope of outpatient specialist care are provided in specialist clinics in cases resulting from the health condition (also at the patient's home) by a doctor with appropriate qualifications or by another person who is authorized to provide specialist services. Guaranteed services include diagnostic and therapeutic activities (including testing of collected biological material) to the extent necessary to provide the service in accordance with current medical knowledge. Guaranteed services are provided in the case of: - specialist advice, - diagnostic tests, - outpatient surgical procedures, - chemotherapy services, - other outpatient services. The service provider provides the patient with the following free of charge to the extent necessary to provide guaranteed services: - diagnostic tests; - medicines and medical devices. Outpatient specialist services are provided on the basis of a referral issued by a health insurance physician. A referral is not required for services provided by: - a gynaecologist and obstetrician, - a dentist, - a venereologist, - an oncologist, - a psychiatrist, and in the case of: - people with tuberculosis, - people infected with HIV, - war and military invalids, repressed persons and war veterans, - blind civilian victims of warfare; - people addicted to alcohol, narcotics and psychotropic substances - in the scope of addiction treatment; - eligible soldiers, in the scope of treatment of injuries or diseases acquired while performing tasks outside the country; - injured veterans, in the scope of treatment of injuries or diseases acquired while performing tasks outside the country.

metadata.name: 3.E.13 Number of days of stay of children and adolescents aged 0-18 in health resort hospital and health resort sanatoria
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Number of days per person of stay of children and adolescents aged 0-18 in spa hospitals and health resort sanatoriums.
metadata.methodology: A spa hospital is a spa treatment facility whose tasks include providing a patient referred for spa treatment or spa rehabilitation with: 1) 24-hour health care services in inpatient conditions; 2) 24-hour medical and nursing care; 3) procedures specified in the treatment program; 4) use of natural medicinal resources and spa treatment facilities; 5) health education.

metadata.name: 3.E.14 Persons practicing sports in sport clubs per 1 thous. population
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Number of people exercising in sports clubs per thousand inhabitants.
metadata.methodology: A practitioner is a person who actively practices a specific type of sport by systematically participating in training or other forms of sports activities and in sports or recreational events. A sports club is a basic organizational unit conducting sports activities, functioning as a legal person. The data concerns sports clubs operating mainly in the area of competitive sports and student sports clubs and religious clubs, whose basic activity is physical recreation. The source of the data is reporting. Since 2018, the data has been developed using direct estimation, taking into account imputation for units that refused to participate in the study.

metadata.name: 3.E.15 Percentage of recreational and sports facilities adapted to the needs of disabled persons (exercising)
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Share of recreational and sports facilities adapted to the needs of disabled people (exercising) in the total number of sports facilities.
metadata.methodology: A sports facility is an independent, compact set of field devices and buildings intended for sports purposes. Sports facilities include stadiums, pitches, sports halls, gyms, courts, swimming pools, ice rinks. A person exercising is a person who actively practices a specific type of sport by systematically participating in training or in another form of sports activities and in sports or recreational events. The data includes sports facilities adapted to the needs of disabled people taking part in exercises (e.g. a place to exercise, facilities for those exercising). They include facilities adapted to people with movement disabilities, disabled people with a type of disability other than movement (e.g. blind, deaf) and facilities adapted to both types of disabilities.

metadata.name: 3.E.16 Patients treated in-patients in health resort hospitals and inpatient rehabilitation facilities
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Number of patients treated as inpatients in spa hospitals and spa sanatoriums.
metadata.methodology: Spa treatment includes hospitals and sanatoriums operating in the spa area, using primarily the properties of natural medicinal resources and the healing properties of the climate in the areas where they are located in treatment, rehabilitation and prevention. Patients of inpatient treatment (stationary treatment) are people who use 24-hour medical and nursing care in stationary health care facilities, i.e. spa hospitals, spa sanatoriums and preventive care facilities. A spa hospital is a spa treatment facility whose tasks include providing a patient who has been referred for spa treatment or spa rehabilitation with: 1) 24-hour health care services in stationary conditions; 2) 24-hour medical and nursing care; 3) procedures provided for in the treatment program; 4) use of natural medicinal resources and spa treatment facilities 5) health education. In addition, a health resort sanatorium for children providing health care services to persons subject to compulsory schooling and education is obliged to provide care services and conditions for teaching and upbringing. The types of diseases for which the sanatorium is intended to treat are established by its regulations.

metadata.name: 3.E.17 Patients treated outpatients in health resort treatment center and and outpatient rehabilitation facilities
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Number of patients treated on an outpatient basis in health resort treatment facilities and in-patient medical rehabilitation facilities.
metadata.methodology: Outpatient health services are the provision of primary or specialist health care and medical rehabilitation services, provided in conditions that do not require their provision in a stationary and 24-hour mode in an appropriately equipped, permanent room. These services may be provided in the premises of the enterprise, including in a vehicle intended for the provision of these services, or at the patient's place of stay. Spa treatment is an organized activity consisting in the provision of health care services in the field of spa treatment or spa rehabilitation, conducted in a spa by spa treatment facilities or outside the spa in hospitals and sanatoriums located in equipped underground mining excavations, using natural conditions.

metadata.name: 3.E.2 Life expectancy - females
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Life expectancy expresses the average number of years that in the current mortality conditions a new born person is expected to continue to live.
metadata.methodology: Average (mean) life expectancy  is the indicator calculated on the basis of a unified methodology recommended by the WHO. Life expectancy – a basic element of the life table.The life tables consist of several functions of age, mathematically interrelated (including the number of survivors, number of decedents, and probability of dying, life expectancy), describing theoretical process of population extinction as the population ages. Values of these functions are calculated on the basis of the age and sex specific death and population rates, observed during a given period (usually 1 year).Life tables  are also used to assess population state of health. Thanks to the observation of mortality trends on the basis of life tables, demographic projections are possible.

metadata.name: 3.E.3 Healthy Life Years — males
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Expected years of life in health is a coefficient reflecting the health situation of the population, calculated on the basis of life tables and individually and subjectively perceived disability.
metadata.methodology: The indicator is interpreted as the expected average number of years that a person aged x completed years will expect to live without disability, provided that current conditions of mortality and health of the population remain at their current level.

metadata.name: 3.E.4 Healthy Life Years — females
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Expected years of life in health is a coefficient reflecting the health situation of the population, calculated on the basis of life tables and individually and subjectively perceived disability.
metadata.methodology: The indicator is interpreted as the expected average number of years that a person aged x completed years will expect to live without disability, provided that current conditions of mortality and health of the population remain at their current level.

metadata.name: 3.E.5 Number of influenza cases and suspected influenza cases per 100 population
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Influenza cases and suspected influenza cases per 100 inhabitants.
metadata.methodology: Morbidity is the frequency of new cases of a disease in the studied population. Influenza is an acute infectious disease of the respiratory system caused by infection with the influenza virus. Influenza is transmitted between people by droplets (for example, when sneezing), and the highest number of cases occurs during seasonal epidemics, causing acute symptoms that prevent professionally active people from working.

metadata.name: 3.E.6 Number of cases of bacterial meningitis and/or encephalitis per 100 thous. population
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Cases of bacterial meningitis and/or encephalitis per 100 thousand population.
metadata.methodology: Bacterial meningitis is a disease of the central nervous system caused by the invasion of bacteria into the meninges. It presents with fever, vomiting, meningeal syndrome, and changes in the cerebrospinal fluid.

metadata.name: 3.E.7 Number of hepatitis C cases per 100 thous. population
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Hepatitis C cases per 100,000 population.
metadata.methodology: Morbidity is the frequency of new cases of a disease in a studied population. Hepatitis C is an infectious disease caused by the Hepatitis C Virus (HCV). The HCV virus is found in the blood and other tissues of an infected person. Infection occurs through contact with infected blood in the event of a break in the continuity of the tissues, e.g. a puncture, a cut in the skin or mucous membrane, or contact with objects that have been contaminated with infected blood.

metadata.name: 3.E.8 Number of cases of invasive infections (Streptococcus pneumoniae) per 10 thous. population
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Invasive Pneumococcal Disease cases per 10 thousand population
metadata.methodology: Morbidity is the frequency of new cases of a disease in a studied population. Invasive Pneumococcal Disease is caused by the Streptococcus pneumoniae bacteria, which causes many clinical syndromes depending on the site of infection (e.g. acute otitis media, pneumonia, sepsis, meningitis). The course of the disease varies from moderately severe, often recurrent upper respiratory tract infections to severe invasive pneumonia with sepsis with a complicated course. Infection is usually preceded by colonization of the upper respiratory tract by S. pneumomiae.

metadata.name: 3.E.9 Number of Lyme disease cases per 10 thous. population
metadata.priority: Development of health prevention and rehabilitation and health promotion
metadata.definition: Lyme disease cases per 10 thousand population.
metadata.methodology: Morbidity is the frequency of new cases of a disease in a studied population. Lyme disease (Latin: borreliosis, morbus Lyme) is a multisystemic infectious disease caused by bacteria belonging to spirochetes. The most common genospecies that are the etiological factor are: Borrelia burgdorferi sensu stricto, Borrelia garinii and Borrelia afzelii, transmitted to humans and some animals by ticks of the genus Ixodes. Human infection occurs after the tick bites and feeds on the skin. The reservoir of the spirochete is numerous species of animals, mainly rodents.

metadata.name: 3.F.2 Share of green areas in the total area
metadata.priority: Healthy human environment
metadata.definition: The area of the existing wild dumps containing municipal waste inventoried by municipalities per 100km² of total area.
metadata.methodology: The area of the existing illegal dumps shows the illegal dumps existing in the commune, ie the area of places not intended for waste storage, on which municipal waste is dumped, inventoried as of December 31.

metadata.name: 3.F.2 Share of green areas in the total area
metadata.priority: Healthy human environment
metadata.definition: Share of green areas in the total area of Poland.
metadata.methodology: Green areas - areas including technical infrastructure and buildings functionally associated with them, covered with vegetation, located in the village of dense buildings or cities, used for the aesthetic, recreational, therapeutic or shielding purposes, in particular parks, lawns, promenades, boulevards, botanical and zoological gardens, children's playgrounds, historic garden, cemeteries, green areas located near roads in the build up areas, squares, historic fortifications, buildings, landfill sites, airports, railway stations and industrial buildings.

metadata.name: 3.F.3 Emission of gaseous pollutants per thousand inhabitants
metadata.priority: Healthy human environment
metadata.definition: Emissions of gaseous pollutants per thousand inhabitants.
metadata.methodology: Emission of gaseous substances into the atmosphere, the concentration of which exceeds the average content of these substances in clean air, negatively affecting human health and the condition and quality of the environment. Characteristic air pollutants occurring in gaseous form include: sulfur dioxide (SO2, nitrogen oxides (NOx), carbon monoxide (CO), carbon dioxide (CO2), hydrocarbons (CnHm) and so-called 'oxidants'. Oxidants are secondary pollutants, created through photochemical reactions of basic pollutants. They include ozone, nitrogen dioxide, formaldehyde, acrolein and others.

metadata.name: 3.F.4 Emission of particulate matter per capita
metadata.priority: Healthy human environment
metadata.definition: Emissions of dust pollution per capita.
metadata.methodology: Emission of solid particles of macroscopic and colloidal fragmentation into the atmosphere, the concentration of which exceeds the average content of these substances in clean air, negatively affecting human health and the state and quality of the environment. Dust pollution is divided depending on the grain size into: dusts of macroscopic fragmentation with grain dimensions from 1 to 10002 m and dusts of colloidal fragmentation with grain dimensions from 0.001 to 12 m. Depending on the source of the dust or the form of its occurrence, the division is used into: dispersive dusts, i.e. those created as a result of mechanical fragmentation of solids (e.g. coal dust during crushing and grinding of coal in power plants) and condensation dusts, created as a result of condensation and solidification of vapors of various chemical substances (e.g. soot), occurring only in the class of colloidal fragmentation. The formation of dust pollution is inextricably linked to all production processes and combustion processes. Particularly large amounts of dust are generated during the combustion of solid fuels. The amount and characteristics of dust generated during the combustion of solid fuels depend on: 1) type of fuel – degree of fragmentation, content and mineralogical composition of ash, caking ability, content of volatile parts, humidity, etc., 2) combustion conditions – type of grate, thermal intensity of the combustion chamber, combustion temperature, air and exhaust flow conditions, etc. In addition, metallurgical processes and the production of building materials, especially cement production, are also 'dust-generating'. Dust pollution includes dust from the combustion of fuels, cement-lime and refractory materials, silicon, artificial fertilizers, coal-graphite and soot, brown coal, surfactants and polymers, as well as particularly dangerous dust pollution such as: chromium, mercury, lead, cadmium, arsenic, zinc, manganese, etc. Particularly toxic dusts also include aromatic hydrocarbons (including carcinogenic benzopyrene). The degree of harmfulness of dusts is determined by their concentration in the atmosphere, chemical and mineralogical composition. Of the mineralogical dusts, quartz is the most harmful.

metadata.name: 3.F.5 Share of population living in areas covered by revitalization processes in the total population
metadata.priority: Healthy human environment
metadata.definition: Share of gminas’ population which carried out revitalization activities on the basis of the gmina revitalisation programme or local revitalisation programme in the total population of urban gminas.
metadata.methodology: Revitalisation - the process of deriving from crisis state degraded areas, conducted in a comprehensive manner, through integrated activities for the local community, space and economy, territorially focused, carried out by stakeholders of revitalisation, based on activities prepared by gminas (municipal, commune) and conducted on the basis of a strategic document (gmina revitalisation programme, revitalisation programme or another strategic document).  Revitalisationa area is an area covering the entire degraded area or a selected part thereof, characterized by a particular concentration of adverse conditions, at least social and one of an economic or environmental or technical or spatial and functional nature, in which the gmina intends to carry out revitalisation due to significant importance for the local development. A revitalisation area should be established on the basis of the provisions of the gmina council resolution or at least indicated in the strategic documents adopted by the gmina council. In the case of degraded areas adopted pursuant to the Revitalisation Act, such resolutions are an act of local law.

metadata.name: 3.G.1 Calls to the occurrence places of emergency rescue teams
metadata.priority: Supporting the health safety of residents
metadata.definition: Number of medical rescue team trips to the scene of the incident.
metadata.methodology: The place of the event is the place where the event causing the sudden health hazard occurred and the area to which its effects extend. Emergency Medical Teams (EMT) are units of the State Emergency Medical Services system that, outside of the hospital, provide medical assistance to people in a state of sudden health hazard.

metadata.name: 3.G.2 Persons who received health care services at the occurrence place per 1 thous. population
metadata.priority: Supporting the health safety of residents
metadata.definition: Number of people who received health care services at the place of the event per 1,000 people.
metadata.methodology: Healthcare services are activities aimed at preventing, preserving, saving, restoring or improving health, as well as other medical activities resulting from the treatment process or separate provisions regulating the principles of their provision. The place of the event is the place where the event causing the state of sudden health threat occurred and the area to which its effects extend.
